Introduction to Wealth Management

Wealth is not a number; it is ownership of value-storing assets

Page 4 / 34 · What Is Wealth

What is wealth?

Click to continue

Wealth ≠ money.

Wealth = assets that store value.

Wealth ≠ money.

Wealth = assets that store value.

Productive assets that generate cash flows

Equity, real estate, bonds...

Consensus assets that aggregate belief via structural scarcity

Gold, bitcoin, jewelry...

Wealth Management Primer
Conceptual framework

Return Premises

Why certain assets outperform in the long run

Page 5 / 34 · Return Premises
Outperformers Growing cash flows, structural scarcity
EquityProductive asset
  • Growing cash-flow ownership
Real EstateProductive asset
  • Growing rental income with location scarcity
GoldConsensus asset
  • Physical scarcity, 5000+ year narrative
BitcoinConsensus asset
  • Algorithmic scarcity, 17 year narrative (yet to prove itself)
Underperformers Fixed or no cash flows, no scarcity story
BondsProductive asset
  • Fixed cash flows (coupon rates)
CommoditiesConsensus asset
  • Supply-demand cycles, lack of sustainable narratives

Construct your personal portfolio of assets

Understand the risks, what to buy and where to invest for each asset class.

Wealth Management Primer
Page 6 / 34

Strategy Choice

Two strategies based on AUM sizes

Page 7 / 34 · Two Strategies

If you start small

Invest regularly in public equities to maximize long-term accumulated return.

If you have extra capital > $1 million

Diversify the portfolio to improve the return earned for each unit of risk.

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: Bloomberg and Morningstar; Williams Asset Management

Major Asset Classes

Public equity can be accessed three ways: pick individual stocks, hold a passive index vehicle, or hire an active manager

Page 8 / 34 · Public Equity

Asset Class

Equity
Public Equity
Alternatives
Gold
Bitcoin
Bond
Commodity

Pick your own

Single stock

Buy individual company shares directly. Full control over selection and timing; no fund layer between you and the company.

Key trade-off

No fund fees, but you bear concentration risk and the risk of making the wrong bet, or making the right bet at the wrong time.

Track the index

Passive

Vehicles built to replicate an index, including ETFs and index mutual funds. Transparent rules, broad diversification, low cost.

Key trade-off

Accepts market return (no alpha attempt). In efficient markets this is the bar most active managers fail to clear.

Hire a manager

Active

Active ETFs and active mutual funds use manager discretion or systematic signals aimed at delivering excess return.

Key trade-off

Pays for the chance of outperformance, but how high is that chance after fees and taxes?

Wealth Management Primer
Source: Wealth Management Primer team analysis · Categorization for educational purposes

Major Asset Classes

Most active funds underperform the index, and it gets worse the longer you look

Page 9 / 34 · Public Equity

Asset Class

Equity
Public Equity
Alternatives
Gold
Bitcoin
Bond
Commodity

Key Takeaways

Europe is hardest97% of EUR-denominated Europe-equity funds trailed the S&P Europe 350 over 10 years.
US close behind86% of US large-cap funds trailed the S&P 500 over 10 years; 90% over 15 years.
Japan offers more roomActive funds did better in Japan (78% trailed at 10Y), but still a clear minority beat the index.
Implication: DIY stock-picking faces the same headwind, without a fund's diversification.
Basis of preparation: SPIVA methodology, S&P Dow Jones Indices. Returns are net of fees; funds that merged or liquidated during the period are counted as underperforming (survivorship-adjusted). Headline statistic is count-based across active funds at the start of each horizon. Comparison indices: S&P 500 (US Large-Cap), S&P Europe 350 (Europe Equity, EUR), S&P Japan 500 (All Japanese Equity). Europe 15Y not reported in source.
Wealth Management Primer
Source: S&P Dow Jones Indices SPIVA Scorecard, as of Dec 31, 2025

Major Asset Classes

For recent graduates, start simple: automate salary into tax-advantaged public equity accounts first

Page 10 / 34 · Public Equity

Asset Class

Equity
Public Equity
Alternatives
Gold
Bitcoin
Bond
Commodity

1. Capture employer match

Action: contribute to your 401(k) at least up to the full company match.

It is part of your salary package; skipping it is leaving compensation unused.

2. Max Roth IRA

2026 quota: $7,500 under age 50, subject to income eligibility.

Use it for broad equity ETFs or low-cost index funds; qualified withdrawals are tax-free.

3. Add taxable brokerage

Action: automate monthly buys after emergency cash and retirement contributions.

Keep it tax-efficient: broad ETFs, low turnover, and avoid frequent realized gains.

4. Keep it boring

Default: target-date fund, total-market ETF, or S&P 500 ETF.

Avoid turning early salary into concentrated employer stock or speculative trades.

Practical order of operations

401(k)
Match first
Roth IRA
$7,500
Taxable
After that
  • Build emergency cash first: 3-6 months of expenses before taking market risk aggressively.
  • Automate on payday: invest before lifestyle inflation absorbs the raise.
  • Graduate to an RIA later: useful when tax, equity comp, cross-border, or estate complexity becomes real.
Wealth Management Primer
Source: IRS 2026 IRA contribution limits; Wealth Management Primer team analysis

Major Asset Classes

Equity alternatives: hedge funds seek differentiated return streams, but require careful manager selection and risk controls

Page 11 / 34 · Hedge Funds

Asset Class

Hedge Funds
PE / VC

Bitcoin

Commodity

Rule of thumb for selecting hedge fund products

Hedge funds can diversify equity risk, but product selection matters more than the label.

  • Check historical returns, especially drawdowns; drawdowns should be lower than annualized returns.
  • Ask for after-fee returns, since management and performance fees can materially reduce investor outcomes.
  • Diversify across multiple funds to reduce manager and strategy risk; this can be achieved through a fund of funds.
Wealth Management Primer
Sources: Hedge Fund Research / FT; supplied HFRI strategy return tables

Public Alternative Funds

Where to invest for alternative equity: publicly listed vehicles - watch out for premium and liquidity risk

Page 14 / 34 · Listed Vehicles

Asset Class

Equity
Public Equity
Alternatives
Hedge Funds
PE / VC
Gold
Bitcoin
Bond
Commodity

Risk call-out: premium and liquidity

Closed-end funds and listed private-market wrappers can trade far above or below NAV. Paying a premium means future returns must overcome fund performance and possible premium compression; liquidity can vanish when sentiment reverses while private holdings remain hard to value or sell.

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: Robinhood, Fundrise, Pershing Square USA, NYLI, iMGP, Goldman Sachs

Major Asset Classes

Invest in gold only if you have a long-term strategy or in regime-shift periods

Page 15 / 34 · Gold

Asset Class

Equity
Gold
Bitcoin
Bond
Commodity

Retail DCA vehicle for gold exposure

For regular investing, use listed physical gold ETFs or ETCs designed to track spot gold directly, ideally backed by allocated bullion and priced close to the local spot gold benchmark. Examples include GLD, IAU, GLDM, and SGOL.

  • Prefer spot-linked physical gold ETFs/ETCs over gold miners, broad commodity funds, futures funds, leveraged products, or structured notes.
  • Check tracking error, expense ratio, liquidity, bid-ask spread, tax treatment, and whether units are physically backed.
  • DCA can smooth entry timing, but it does not remove gold's volatility or currency exposure.
Wealth Management Primer
Sources: Wind, Bloomberg, JST database, BIS, OECD, CICC

Cryptocurrency · Bitcoin

Most retail investors cannot stomach holding through the swings of cryptocurrency price

Page 17 / 34 · Bonus · Crypto

Asset Class

Equity
Gold
Bitcoin
Bond
Commodity

Pick your stance, then size accordingly.

  • If it's a diversifying asset → dollar-cost average, cap at 1-3%
  • If you don't believe it diversifies → don't invest
  • If you're speculating → size only what you can lose

Bottom line

Build slowly, keep it small, never time the market, don't use leverage.

Major Asset Classes · Fixed Income · Access

Retail investors access fixed income through four different routes

Page 20 / 34 · Access
1

Individual bonds via direct issuer (e.g. U.S. Treasury Department)

Where: TreasuryDirect.gov. Electronic-only accounts; no broker, no markup. Lowest-cost route for taxable government exposure.

Marketable Treasuries: $100 minimum; electronic savings bonds: $25 minimum

2

Individual bonds via brokerage platforms

Where: Fidelity, Schwab, Interactive Brokers, Vanguard bond and Certificate of Deposit desks. Inventory varies; secondary market is fragmented.

Typical lot: $1,000 face per bond; minimums vary by issue and broker

3

ETFs and mutual funds

Examples:

  • Core aggregate bond index = AGG, BND
  • Investment grade corporate bonds = LQD
  • High yield bonds = HYG
  • Long-duration treasury = TLT
  • Money market funds = SPAXX, VMFXX, SWVXX

Low minimums, daily liquidity; most do not mature to par

4

Ladders & wrappers (e.g. target-maturity ETFs, broker-built ladders, Separately Managed Accounts, or advisory portfolios)

Where: Brokerage tools, advisors, private banks, and defined-maturity ETF suites (iBonds, BulletShares).

Good for planning; still check fees, liquidity, calls, and underlying holdings

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: TreasuryDirect.gov; iShares, Invesco, Fidelity, Vanguard product pages; FDIC brokered CD guidance

Major Asset Classes · Fixed Income · Hold to Maturity

Take advantage of “hold-to-maturity” protection

Page 21 / 34 · Hold to Maturity
What is hold-to-maturity (YTM)

Buy an individual bond and keep it until the issuer repays principal on the stated maturity date — collecting the scheduled coupons along the way.

Assumptions · the protection holds only if all three are true
1

No credit event like default.

2

No call or prepayment surprise.

3

No forced sale.

The benefit

Under those three conditions, your realized return by holding the bond until maturity is very close to the yield-to-maturity (YTM) quoted at purchase — interim price swings don't change what lands in your account.

→ See Appendix for why realized return isn't exactly YTM (reinvestment-rate risk).
Example. Buy this US Treasury Note at the quoted price and 6.423% YTM is the annualized return you lock in — provided the three assumptions hold.
Wealth Management Primer
Sources: FINRA bond basics; SEC Investor.gov; brokerage bond list screenshot

Major Asset Classes · Fixed Income · TLT vs. Treasury

A 20-year Treasury yielding 5.077% does not mean the 20-Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) will pay you 5.077%

Page 22 / 34 · TLT Detail
Trading bonds = a bet on long-rate direction

5.077% is what a 20-year Treasury pays you IF you buy direct and hold to maturity. TLT, which holds the same 20-year Treasuries but never matures, has delivered roughly −13% cumulatively over the past decade. Same underlying bonds; the wrapper is the bet.

1

Long-rate forecasting is harder than picking Nvidia.

2

TLT never reaches a maturity date.

3

Duration converts rate-views into equity-like P&L.

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: MarketWatch TMUBMUSD20Y (May 24, 2026); iShares TLT product page (May 22, 2026); SEC Investor.gov on bond ETFs

Section 3

Fees and Taxes

Costs, withholding tax, fund domicile, and estate-tax exposure can materially change investor outcomes.

Wealth Management Primer
Page 23 / 34

Carver, Smart Portfolios (2017)

Returns are uncertain — costs are not. Five non-market levers quietly compound

Page 24 / 34 · Non-market factors

The Five Levers You Control

1

Vehicles

ETF · Direct shares · Active fund · Synthetic ETN

2

Broker / Platform

Minimum commissions · Platform fees · Stamp duty

3

Tax Wrapper

ISA · SIPP · 401(k) · IRA

4

Trading Behaviour

Turnover · Limit orders · Order splitting

5

Portfolio Construction

Top-down handcrafting · Diversification breakeven

The "Rule of 20"

1% annual cost ≈ a £20k upfront cheque on a £100k portfolio.

Multiply any ongoing annual cost by ~20 to see its present-value drag over a typical investing lifetime.

What does that mean in dollars?

$100,000 invested for 30 years at 8% pre-cost return:

Annual costFinal valueLost vs no cost
0 bp$1,006,266—
10 bp$978,686−$27,580 (2.7%)
50 bp$875,496−$130,770 (13%)
100 bp$761,226−$245,040 (24%)
200 bp$574,349−$431,917 (43%)
Wealth Management Primer
Source: Carver, R. (2017), Smart Portfolios; compound drag illustration calculated at $100k, 30 yrs, 8% pre-cost.

Active Fund Fee Drag

Actively managed The Growth Fund of America (AGTHX) outperformed at NAV — but load, expenses, and tax flip the investor's final result vs S&P 500 index tracker (SPY)

Where a US taxable investor's 10-year (2016 – 2025) return actually goes

Page 25 / 34 · AGTHX

Notes: NAV = Net Asset Value (return after fund expense ratio, before the front-end sales load). MOP = Maximum Offering Price (return after both expense ratio and the 5.75% front-end load). AGTHX 10-yr returns (Capital Group Q4 2025 fact sheet): 15.15% at NAV; 14.47% at MOP. AGTHX gross 15.74% = NAV + 0.59% expense ratio (DERIVED). Load drag = NAV − MOP = 0.68 pp/yr (DERIVED). AGTHX tax drag 1.95 pp/yr = Class F-2 (no-load) 10-yr before-tax (13.91%) − after-tax-on-distributions (11.96%) from AGTHX summary prospectus (12/31/2024, highest US federal individual rate, no state/local tax), applied as representative-year proxy. SPY net-to-investor 14.26% = S&P 500 14.82% − SPY expense 0.0945% − SPY tax drag 0.47 pp/yr (SPY prospectus 12/31/2024). AGTHX outperformance vs S&P 500 at NAV (+0.33 pp/yr) is on the SPIVA basis (after fund expenses, before load and tax).

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: AGTHX Summary Prospectus; Capital Group Q4 2025 Fact Sheet; SPY Prospectus; SPIVA U.S. Year-End 2024 Scorecard.

Tax considerations for non-residents

If you're not a US tax resident — or if you are, but not US-domiciled — two US tax rates can quietly cost you tens of thousands of dollars

Page 28 / 34 · The Hook

Withholding Tax

30%

of every US-stock dividend, withheld at source. Treaty rates can reduce this to 15%, 10%, or 0% — depending on your country.

Estate Tax

40%

on US-situs assets above just $60,000 — at death. 15 countries have estate-tax treaties that raise the exemption or lower the rate.

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: IRC §871(a); IRC §2001(c); IRS Form 706-NA

Tax considerations for non-residents · 1 of 2

Withholding tax: a 30% dividend drag that compounds year after year — unless you choose the right fund domicile

Page 29 / 34 · Withholding

30%

on US-source dividends Applies to NRAs holding US-domiciled funds or stocks. Largely permanent — no reclaim.

  • Treaty rate drops to 15% for most major treaty countries (UK, Germany, Japan, Australia, Canada).
  • Common workaround: Irish-domiciled UCITS ETFs — Ireland has a 15% US treaty, so the fund (not you) is taxed at 15% at source.
  • Compounding matters: the gap is small per year but grows quickly over a 20-year horizon.

Irish-domiciled ETF advantage

0.20%

lower annual drag vs. holding a US-domiciled S&P 500 ETF

~$23K

more wealth after 20 years on $100K invested ($644K vs $621K)

Illustrative: assumes 1.39% S&P 500 dividend yield and 10% gross total return. Compounding amplifies the gap.

Cheapest S&P 500 ETF — head-to-head SPYM US-domiciled · distributing SPY5 Irish-domiciled · distributing
Total Expense Ratio 0.02% 0.03%
US withholding on dividends 30%final, no reclaim 15%via US-Ireland treaty
Dividend drag
assuming 1.39% S&P 500 yield		 0.42% 0.21%
Total annual drag 0.44% 0.24%
Wealth Management Primer
Sources: IRC §871(a); US-Ireland Treaty Art. 10; SSGA SPYM & SPY5 factsheets (2026); S&P DJI Q4 2025 dividend report

Tax considerations for non-residents · 2 of 2

Estate tax: only the first $60K of US-situs assets is exempt — everything above is taxed at up to 40%

Page 30 / 34 · Estate Tax

Non-resident, non-citizen

$60K

exemption on US-situs assets

The citizen exemption is ~250× larger than the NRA exemption.

US citizen or resident

$15M

basic exclusion (2026)

Top rate above $1M

40%effective since 2013

Threshold last raised

1988not indexed for inflation

Treaty exception · 15 countries

Each of these countries has an estate-tax treaty raising the exemption and/or lowering the rate. Read the specific treaty — terms vary substantially. UK · Germany · France · Japan · Canada · Australia · Netherlands · Switzerland · Italy · Denmark · Ireland · Austria · Finland · Greece · South Africa

Workaround · Foreign corp wrapper

Hold US assets through a foreign corporation (e.g., BVI) where you are the 100% shareholder. Shares in a foreign corp are not US-situs — so they fall outside the US estate-tax net. You → Foreign Corp → US Assets ⚠ Trade-offs: setup & annual costs; CFC/PFIC risk if family are US persons; generally must be in place before acquiring US assets. Don't DIY — get advice.

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: IRS Form 706-NA; IRC §2001(c), §2010(c)(3); Rev. Proc. 2025-32

Tax considerations for non-residents

The two traps overlap — but they don't catch the same people

Page 31 / 34 · Who's Affected

⚠ Simplified illustration

Actual treatment depends on visa status, domicile vs. Substantial Presence Test, intent, treaty, and specific assets held. Many individuals straddle multiple categories during a year. Talk to a tax advisor before you structure.

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: IRS Pub 519; IRS Substantial Presence Test; IRS Form 706-NA

Closing Recap

Key lessons: own value-storing assets, choose implementation carefully, and watch costs

Page 32 / 34 · Recap
Section 1

Wealth = assets that store value (productive asset vs consensus asset).

Section 2
  • Equity: two strategies based on AUM size.
  • Gold & Bitcoin: long-term view, small positions.
  • Bonds: safer than bond funds when held to maturity.
  • Commodities: do not invest as a retail investor.
  • Insurance is not for investment.
Section 3
  • Fees compound: small annual costs become large lifetime drag.
  • Taxes and wrappers matter: account type, domicile, withholding, and estate rules shape after-tax returns.
Wealth Management Primer
Closing recap

Appendix

Asset returns vary if you invest <20 years; starting & ending years matter

Page 33 / 34 · Return Quilt
Wealth Management Primer
Source: Bloomberg and Morningstar

Appendix

Even held to maturity, realized return can undershoot the quoted yield-to-maturity due to reinvestment risk

Page 34 / 34 · YTM Mechanics

Why YTM assumes reinvestment at y

YTM is an internal rate of return calculation. It treats every cash flow as if it compounds at one constant rate y.

For a bond priced at P, with periodic coupons C, face value F, maturity n, and YTM y:

Each future cash flow is discounted at the same rate y. Rearranged, the equation implies that if you invest P today and every coupon reinvests at y, your terminal wealth is consistent with earning y annually.

However, the assumption of reinvesting coupons at the same yield y is rarely true in practice.

Worked example — 3-year annual-pay bond

To actually earn the quoted YTM, the first coupon must compound for 2 years, the second for 1 year, and the final coupon arrives at maturity. Terminal value is then:

This equals what your original P would grow to if it compounded at y for 3 years. If coupons reinvest at lower rates, the terminal value is lower — so the realized yield is lower than the original YTM. Higher reinvestment rates push realized yield above YTM.

Key takeaway

Bigger reinvestment-rate range = more reinvestment risk. Zero-coupon bonds eliminate it entirely — no interim cash flows to reinvest, so realized return locks at YTM if held to maturity.

Wealth Management Primer
Sources: FINRA bond basics; SEC Investor.gov; standard fixed-income textbook treatments
